subject: currentaffairs
title: Inequality of Birth
question: Is it fair that some people are born into wealthy families while others are born into poverty - and that this affects almost every opportunity they'll have in life?
answer: Discussion question — there is no single correct answer. Assess the reasoning, honesty and perspective-taking, not a 'right' response.
modelReasoningPath: What to listen for: Does the child conflate "unfair" with "something that can or should be changed"? Can they think about what a just alternative would actually look like, rather than just agreeing it's wrong?

subject: currentaffairs
title: Should 16-Year-Olds Vote?
question: Should the voting age be lowered to 16? What's your view - and make the case for the other side too.
answer: Discussion question — there is no single correct answer. Assess the reasoning, honesty and perspective-taking, not a 'right' response.
modelReasoningPath: What to listen for: Can the child make the opposing case to their own view as well as their own? Being asked to argue for both sides is a test of intellectual fairness and the ability to understand perspectives you disagree with. Bonus: does the child notice that they personally are close to this age and therefore have a stake in the answer? ---

subject: currentaffairs
title: Social Media: Good or Harm?
question: Do you think social media does more good or more harm for people your age? You've grown up with it - I want your honest view.
answer: Discussion question — there is no single correct answer. Assess the reasoning, honesty and perspective-taking, not a 'right' response.
modelReasoningPath: What to listen for: Does the child draw on personal experience, or only abstract arguments? A child who says "I've noticed in myself that..." is showing a quality top schools treasure: genuine self-reflection. Does the child acknowledge the role that tech companies play in designing products to maximise engagement - and what that means for user "choice"?

subject: currentaffairs
title: Animals and Rights
question: Do you think animals should have rights - and if so, which ones? Are some animals more deserving of rights than others?
answer: Discussion question — there is no single correct answer. Assess the reasoning, honesty and perspective-taking, not a 'right' response.
modelReasoningPath: What to listen for: Does the child think carefully about what a "right" is before deciding whether animals can have them? A right implies someone capable of having interests that can be violated - which connects to questions about consciousness and sentience. A child who says "it depends what you mean by a right" before diving in is reasoning at the right level. ---

subject: currentaffairs
title: School Uniforms
question: Do you think school uniforms are a good idea? Make the strongest case for the opposite of whatever you actually believe.
answer: Discussion question — there is no single correct answer. Assess the reasoning, honesty and perspective-taking, not a 'right' response.
modelReasoningPath: What to listen for: Can the child genuinely argue against themselves? This is the most important intellectual skill in the whole section - the ability to steelman an opposing view. A child who gives a flat, reluctant two-sentence counter-argument has not really done it. A child who says "actually, now that I've argued the other side, I'm less sure than I was" has shown exactly the kind of thinking top schools want to cultivate. --- ## Quick Reference | # | Question | Section | Key Tension | |---|----------|---------|-------------| | A1 | The News Opener | Current Affairs | World awareness, honest opinion | | A2 | One Problem to Solve | Current Affairs | Prioritisation, global empathy | | A3 | Richer & Poorer Countries | Current Affairs | National interest vs. global responsibility | | A4 | Is It Ever Right to Break the Law? | Current Affairs | Legal vs. moral obligation | | A5 | Britain & the Wider World | Current Affairs | Geopolitical awareness | | B1 | Excited or Worried About AI? | Technology | Holding two views simultaneously | | B2 | AI and Cheating | Technology | What learning is actually for | | B3 | Can Computers Be Creative? | Technology | Defining creativity | | B4 | Job a Robot Could Never Do | Technology | Capability vs. accountability | | C1 | Who Should Act on Climate? | Climate | Systemic vs. individual agency | | C2 | Intergenerational Fairness | Climate | Fairness vs. responsibility | | C3 | Personal Sacrifice | Climate | Honest self-reflection | | D1 | Five Strangers or One Friend | Moral Dilemma | Utilitarian vs. relational ethics | | D2 | When Lying is Right | Moral Dilemma | Absolute vs. contextual morality | | D3 | Best Friend's Cheating | Moral Dilemma | Loyalty vs. fairness | | D4 | Motive vs. Outcome | Moral Dilemma | Consequentialism vs. virtue ethics | | D5 | The Found Money | Moral Dilemma | Honesty about actual behaviour | | E1 | Inequality of Birth | Society | Luck, fairness, redistribution | | E2 | Voting Age | Society | Civic rights, maturity | | E3 | Social Media | Society | Personal experience vs. systemic design | | E4 | Animal Rights | Society | Defining rights, sentience | | E5 | School Uniforms | Society | Arguing against yourself | --- Version 1.0 - Current Affairs & Moral Thinking
